title: Chainsaw Fails to Start
shortDescription: The chainsaw cranks or pulls without firing, or requires excessive effort to start. This is one of the most common issues across all Stihl chainsaw models.
fullDescription:
A Stihl chainsaw that refuses to start typically points to a failure in one of three systems: ignition, fuel delivery, or compression. The ignition system includes the spark plug, ignition module (coil), and flywheel. The fuel system includes the carburetor, fuel lines, primer bulb, and fuel filter. Compression issues can stem from a worn piston, damaged cylinder, or a decompression valve that is stuck open.

Before attempting repairs, confirm the chain brake is released, the choke is set correctly, and the stop switch is in the RUN position. Many no-start calls are resolved by simply correcting operator error. On models with M-Tronic electronics (MS 271, MS 291, MS 462), the engine management system may need to relearn fuel settings after carburetor work or fuel changes.

Stale fuel is the leading cause of no-start on chainsaws stored for more than 30 days. Ethanol-blended fuel absorbs moisture and degrades carburetor jets within weeks. Always drain fuel before storage or use a fuel stabilizer like Stihl MotoMix. If old fuel is present, drain the tank and carburetor completely before attempting to diagnose further.

If the engine floods during cold-start attempts, remove the spark plug, pull the rope 6-8 times to clear fuel, reinstall the plug, and restart with the choke fully off.

causes.cause: Fouled or failed spark plug
Stale or contaminated fuel clogging the carburetor
Clogged fuel filter or cracked fuel line
Failed ignition coil or incorrect coil gap
Flooded engine from incorrect starting procedure

symptoms: Engine cranks but does not fire
Engine fires briefly then dies
Excessive rope pull required with no start
Smell of fuel at exhaust but no ignition
Spark plug is wet with fuel after failed start attempts
Engine starts cold but will not restart when hot

fixSteps.title: Check and replace the spark plug
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the spark plug using a plug wrench. Inspect for fouling, cracking, or electrode wear. Gap should be 0.5 mm (0.020 in). Replace with NGK BPMR7A or Bosch WSB6F if worn or wet.

fixSteps.title: Drain and replace fuel
fixSteps.instruction: Drain old fuel from the tank completely. Refill with fresh 50:1 mixture using Stihl HP Ultra oil or equivalent. Purge old fuel from the carburetor by running until stall after draining.

fixSteps.title: Inspect fuel filter and lines
fixSteps.instruction: Use a bent wire hook to pull the fuel filter from the tank. Replace if discolored or blocked. Inspect fuel lines for cracks, brittleness, or kinks. Replace lines if any damage found.

fixSteps.title: Clean or rebuild the carburetor
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the air filter and carburetor. Disassemble the Walbro or Zama carburetor and clean all jets and passages with carburetor cleaner. Install a rebuild kit (diaphragm, needle, gaskets) and reassemble. Adjust L and H screws to factory spec.

fixSteps.title: Test ignition coil and gap
fixSteps.instruction: Use a spark tester to verify spark at the plug. Set ignition coil air gap to 0.2 mm using a feeler gauge or business card. If no spark with correct gap, replace the ignition module.

whenToCallPro: If the chainsaw still won't start after replacing the spark plug, cleaning the carburetor, and verifying spark — especially if you suspect low compression or internal engine damage.

faq.q: Why does my Stihl MS 180 start then die immediately?
faq.a: This usually means the choke is partially closed or the carburetor idle circuit is blocked. Try running with choke fully open and clean the idle jet if it stalls at low RPM.

faq.q: Can I use regular gasoline in my Stihl chainsaw?
faq.a: Stihl recommends 89-octane or higher, mixed 50:1 with Stihl HP or HP Ultra 2-stroke oil. Fuel with more than 10% ethanol (E15, E85) can damage the carburetor and fuel lines.

faq.q: How do I clear a flooded Stihl chainsaw engine?
faq.a: Remove the spark plug, move the choke to OPEN and the switch to RUN, and pull the rope 6-8 times to clear the combustion chamber. Reinstall the plug and try starting with no choke.

faq.q: What is the correct spark plug gap for Stihl chainsaws?
faq.a: Most Stihl chainsaws use a 0.5 mm (0.020 in) gap. Check your model's manual for exact specification before gapping a new plug.

title: Chainsaw Stalls or Dies When Cutting
shortDescription: The chainsaw runs at idle but bogs, stalls, or dies as soon as the throttle is applied or the chain contacts wood. This indicates a lean fuel condition or air leak.
fullDescription:
A chainsaw that stalls under load is most often caused by insufficient fuel reaching the engine at high RPM. The high-speed needle (H screw) on the carburetor controls fuel mixture under load. If set too lean, the engine starves for fuel the moment power demand increases. Dirty fuel filters, cracked fuel lines, or a deteriorated carburetor diaphragm can all produce the same symptom by restricting fuel volume.

Air leaks in the intake system mimic a lean condition because unmetered air enters beyond the carburetor. Common leak points include the crankcase seals, impulse line fitting, carburetor gaskets, and decompression valve. Even small air leaks cause violent stalling under load, erratic idle, and hard starting.

On M-Tronic equipped models (MS 271, MS 291, MS 462, MS 500i), the electronic fuel management compensates automatically for altitude and temperature. If the M-Tronic system has an active fault, self-correction stops and lean stalling can result. Reset M-Tronic by pressing and holding the throttle trigger for 3 seconds at full-throttle WOT before cutting.

A clogged spark arrestor screen in the muffler can also cause under-load stalling by creating back-pressure. Remove the muffler cover and clean or replace the screen — this is often overlooked during diagnosis.

causes.cause: Carburetor high-speed needle set too lean
Clogged or failing carburetor diaphragm reducing fuel volume
Air leak at carburetor gasket or crankcase seal
Clogged spark arrestor screen in muffler
Plugged fuel filter restricting flow at high demand

symptoms: Runs fine at idle but bogs when throttle is applied
Dies immediately when the chain contacts wood
Engine surges at part throttle
Restarts easily but stalls again under load
Black or sooty exhaust at idle, clean at wide-open throttle

fixSteps.title: Clean the spark arrestor screen
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the muffler cover and the muffler. Extract the spark arrestor screen. Clean carbon buildup with a wire brush or replace the screen if perforated. Reassemble and test.

fixSteps.title: Replace the fuel filter
fixSteps.instruction: Pull the fuel filter from the tank using a wire hook. Replace it with a new Stihl OEM filter. Inspect fuel lines for cracks while the tank is accessible.

fixSteps.title: Adjust the carburetor high-speed needle
fixSteps.instruction: With engine fully warm, advance throttle to WOT while cutting. If the engine stumbles, turn the H screw counterclockwise in 1/8-turn increments until it pulls cleanly. Maximum RPM per model spec must not be exceeded.

fixSteps.title: Rebuild or replace the carburetor
fixSteps.instruction: Disassemble the carburetor and inspect the main diaphragm and metering diaphragm for cracks, stiffness, or holes. Install a complete rebuild kit. Set H and L screws to factory base settings before starting.

fixSteps.title: Pressure-test for air leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Seal the intake and exhaust ports. Apply 8 PSI compressed air to the crankcase via the decompression port. Spray soapy water around all gaskets, seals, and fittings. Bubbles reveal leak points. Replace any leaking seals or gaskets.

repairTime: 30 minutes to 3 hours depending on root cause
whenToCallPro: If the chainsaw stalls under load after carburetor rebuild and fuel system inspection, suspect an air leak in the crankcase seals — this requires pressure testing equipment and seal replacement best handled by a Stihl dealer.
videoSearchQuery: stihl chainsaw dies under load bogs fix

faq.q: My Stihl MS 271 bogs at full throttle — could it be M-Tronic?
faq.a: Yes. The M-Tronic system can learn an incorrect fuel map if started repeatedly at incorrect settings. Reset by running at full throttle for 3 seconds in open air before cutting.

faq.q: Will a dirty air filter cause stalling under load?
faq.a: Yes. A clogged air filter starves the engine of air, causing a rich condition at idle but insufficient airflow at WOT. Always inspect the air filter before deeper diagnosis.

faq.q: What is the H screw on a Stihl carburetor?
faq.a: The H (high speed) needle controls the fuel/air mixture at wide-open throttle. Turning it counterclockwise richens the mixture; clockwise leans it. Factory base setting is typically 1 full turn out from gently seated.

title: Bar and Chain Oil Not Reaching the Bar
shortDescription: The guide bar and chain are running dry, causing excessive heat, rapid wear, and potential chain seizure. Oil tank empties slowly or not at all.
fullDescription:
Bar and chain lubrication is critical for safe chainsaw operation. Stihl chainsaws use a worm-gear driven oil pump to deliver oil from the reservoir through an internal passage to an outlet port on the bar mount. The chain carries this oil along the bar groove and around the sprocket nose. If lubrication fails, the bar can glow red, the chain stretches rapidly, and the sprocket nose can seize within minutes of cutting.

The oil delivery system can fail at several points: the oil filter pickup in the tank can clog, the oil line between the tank and pump can crack or kink, the oil pump worm gear can strip, or the oil outlet port on the saw body can plug with sawdust and debris. The oil pump output adjuster screw (if present) may also be set to minimum.

On professional models like the MS 462 and MS 500i, the oil pump is adjustable. Turning the output screw counterclockwise increases oil flow. Always set oil output to maximum when cutting hardwood or in warm conditions. After bar removal for maintenance, ensure the oil port in the bar is aligned with the outlet on the saw body.

A quick field test: hold the running chainsaw 10–15 cm above a light-colored surface (cardboard or paper) at fast idle for 10 seconds. A visible oil streak confirms lubrication. No streak means the system is blocked or the pump has failed.

causes.cause: Clogged oil pickup filter inside the oil tank
Cracked or kinked oil delivery line
Failed or stripped oil pump worm gear
Plugged oil outlet port on saw body

symptoms: Bar and chain run hot and smoke during cutting
Oil tank level does not decrease during use
Chain wears or dulls unusually fast
No oil streak visible on paper test
Bar groove becomes dry and discolored

fixSteps.title: Perform the paper test
fixSteps.instruction: Hold the running saw 10-15 cm above a piece of cardboard. Run at fast idle for 10 seconds. An oil streak confirms the pump is delivering oil. No streak means the system is blocked or the pump has failed.

fixSteps.title: Clean the oil outlet port
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the bar and clean the oil outlet port hole on the saw body with a small pick or compressed air. Clear any sawdust compaction from the bar oil hole slot as well.

fixSteps.title: Replace the oil filter and inspect oil line
fixSteps.instruction: Open the oil cap and use a wire hook to retrieve the oil pickup filter. Replace if clogged. Trace the oil line from the tank to the pump and replace if cracked, kinked, or brittle.

fixSteps.title: Increase oil pump output
fixSteps.instruction: On adjustable models, locate the oil output adjuster screw (usually behind the sprocket cover). Turn counterclockwise to increase oil flow. Set to maximum for demanding cuts.

fixSteps.title: Replace the oil pump
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the sprocket cover, clutch drum, and clutch. Extract the worm gear and oil pump assembly. Inspect for stripped gear teeth. Install a new pump and worm gear, reassemble the clutch and sprocket cover.

whenToCallPro: If the oil line and filter are intact but the pump still does not deliver oil, the internal worm gear is likely stripped and requires clutch disassembly. Have a dealer inspect if unfamiliar with clutch removal.
videoSearchQuery: stihl chainsaw bar oil not feeding fix

faq.q: Can I use vegetable oil as bar oil in a pinch?
faq.a: Yes, in an emergency. Vegetable oil provides adequate lubrication and is biodegradable, but it gums up internal passages in cold weather. Use proper Stihl ForestPlus or equivalent bar and chain oil for regular use.

faq.q: How often should I check the bar oil level?
faq.a: Check the oil level every time you refuel. A properly functioning system should empty the oil tank at roughly the same rate as the fuel tank when cutting.

faq.q: Why does my bar get hot even when the oil level looks full?
faq.a: A full tank does not confirm oil is reaching the bar. The oil line or outlet port may be blocked. Perform the paper test to verify actual oil delivery.

title: Chain Brake Engaged and Will Not Release
shortDescription: The chain brake is locked in the engaged position, preventing the chain from moving. This is a critical safety system and must be properly functional before use.
fullDescription:
The Stihl chain brake is an inertia- and hand-activated safety device that stops the chain within milliseconds of kickback. It is operated by the front hand guard. When activated, a spring-loaded band clamps around the clutch drum to halt the chain. If this brake will not release after pushing the front guard back toward the front handle, the brake band, spring, or actuating lever may be seized, broken, or contaminated.

Oil and sawdust contamination is the most common cause of a stuck chain brake. Over time, oil mixes with fine sawdust inside the sprocket cover to form a hard, pitch-like buildup that binds the brake band to the drum. This can prevent full engagement or full release. Regular cleaning of the sprocket cover area is essential preventive maintenance.

The chain brake spring can also weaken or break, leaving the brake either permanently engaged or failing to engage on kickback. A failed brake spring is a serious safety hazard — a chainsaw must never be operated with a malfunctioning chain brake. Replace the spring immediately if the brake lacks tension or snaps unevenly.

After any repair to the chain brake system, verify operation before cutting: with the saw stopped, push the front guard forward to engage the brake, then release. The guard must snap back smartly. Repeat 3 times to confirm reliable operation.

causes.cause: Sawdust and oil buildup binding the brake band
Broken or weakened chain brake spring
Bent or damaged brake band
Worn or cracked front hand guard pivot

symptoms: Chain does not move even at full throttle
Front guard cannot be pushed back to release position
Grinding or resistance when attempting to release brake
Chain brake engages spontaneously during normal cutting
Chain brake does not engage when front guard is struck

fixSteps.title: Clean the sprocket cover and brake area
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the sprocket cover. Thoroughly clean all sawdust, oil, and pitch buildup from the brake band, clutch drum surface, and cover interior. Use a stiff brush and parts cleaner. Dry completely before reassembling.

fixSteps.title: Inspect and test brake spring
fixSteps.instruction: With the sprocket cover removed, inspect the chain brake spring for cracks, distortion, or loss of tension. The spring should snap the brake band firmly. Replace if spring lacks force or shows damage.

fixSteps.title: Inspect and replace brake band
fixSteps.instruction: Check the brake band for wear, cracks, or heat discoloration. The friction lining must not be worn below the minimum thickness. Replace the brake band if lining is thin or band is distorted.

fixSteps.title: Verify brake function after reassembly
fixSteps.instruction: Reassemble the sprocket cover and chain. With the saw OFF, push the front guard forward and pull it back 5 times. The brake must engage and release smoothly each time. Then test inertia activation by tapping the guard.

whenToCallPro: The chain brake is a critical safety component. If you are uncertain about proper reassembly or function after cleaning, take the saw to a Stihl dealer for inspection and testing before use.
videoSearchQuery: stihl chainsaw chain brake stuck fix

faq.q: Is it safe to use a chainsaw with the chain brake disabled?
faq.a: No. Operating a chainsaw with a non-functional chain brake is extremely dangerous and illegal in many jurisdictions. Repair or replace the brake before any use.

faq.q: Why does my chain brake activate during normal cutting?
faq.a: Spontaneous activation usually means the inertia weight is too sensitive or the brake spring is too strong after cleaning. More commonly, the saw is experiencing severe vibration from a loose or damaged part.

faq.q: How do I know if the chain brake is engaged?
faq.a: With the saw off, try rotating the chain by hand. If the chain cannot be moved, the brake is engaged. Push the front hand guard firmly rearward toward the front handle to release it.

title: Clutch Drum Worn or Slipping
shortDescription: The clutch drum is scored, worn, or the centrifugal clutch shoes are failing, causing the chain to slip, drag at idle, or lose power under load.
fullDescription:
Stihl chainsaws use a centrifugal clutch that engages the chain only above idle RPM. The clutch consists of shoes (pads) that expand outward at speed to contact the inner surface of the clutch drum, which is directly connected to the sprocket and chain. As the clutch shoes or drum surface wears, slippage occurs — the engine revs but the chain turns slowly or not at all under load.

The reverse failure is also common: worn clutch springs allow the shoes to drag against the drum even at idle, causing the chain to rotate continuously. This is a serious safety hazard because the chain moves whenever the engine runs, regardless of throttle position. If the chain moves at idle, inspect the clutch immediately.

The clutch drum drives the sprocket that guides the chain. A worn sprocket (rivets worn or teeth hooked) causes rapid chain stretch and skipping. Stihl recommends replacing the sprocket every 2-3 chains. Grooves worn into the clutch drum from a worn sprocket can be seen as score marks on the drum interior.

Clutch shoe replacement requires a clutch removal tool and piston stop. The clutch has left-hand threads on most Stihl models — it unscrews clockwise (turning to the right). Mark the thread direction before applying force to avoid damage.

causes.cause: Worn clutch drum interior surface from abrasion
Broken or weak centrifugal clutch springs allowing idle drag
Worn clutch shoes that no longer grip the drum effectively
Worn drive sprocket causing chain slippage

symptoms: Chain rotates continuously at idle
Chain slips or barely moves at full throttle
Burning smell from clutch area during cutting
Chain speed does not match engine speed
Vibration or chattering from the sprocket cover area

fixSteps.title: Inspect the chain at idle
fixSteps.instruction: Start the saw and observe chain movement at idle WITHOUT touching the throttle. If the chain moves at idle, the clutch is dragging due to weak springs or distorted shoes. Shut off immediately and inspect.

fixSteps.title: Remove the sprocket cover and inspect the sprocket
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the bar and chain. Remove the sprocket cover. Inspect the drive sprocket for hooked or worn teeth. Replace the sprocket if tooth profiles are hooked or depth has decreased significantly.

fixSteps.title: Remove and inspect the clutch assembly
fixSteps.instruction: Install the piston stop tool through the spark plug hole. Using the clutch removal wrench, unscrew the clutch CLOCKWISE (left-hand thread). Remove the clutch shoes and spring. Inspect shoes for wear and spring for cracking or distortion.

fixSteps.title: Replace clutch shoes, spring, and drum
fixSteps.instruction: Install new clutch shoes and spring. Inspect the clutch drum interior for scoring. If scored, replace the drum as well. Reinstall the clutch (counterclockwise to tighten — left-hand thread). Torque to spec.

whenToCallPro: Clutch removal requires specialized Stihl tools and knowledge of left-hand thread direction. If you do not have the correct removal wrench and piston stop tool, have a Stihl dealer perform this repair.

faq.q: Why does the Stihl clutch have left-hand threads?
faq.a: Left-hand threads prevent the clutch from loosening during normal operation, since the crankshaft rotation would otherwise unscrew a right-hand threaded fastener.

faq.q: How often should I replace the drive sprocket?
faq.a: Stihl recommends replacing the sprocket every two to three chains. A worn sprocket accelerates chain wear and can damage the clutch drum.

faq.q: Can I run the chainsaw if the chain moves at idle?
faq.a: No. A chain that moves at idle is extremely dangerous. The saw should be shut off immediately and the clutch inspected or replaced before any further use.

title: M-Tronic Electronic Engine Management Fault
shortDescription: The M-Tronic system on equipped Stihl models has lost calibration or developed a fault, causing poor throttle response, stalling, or failure to reach correct RPM.
fullDescription:
M-Tronic is Stihl's microprocessor-based engine management system used on the MS 271, MS 291, MS 362, MS 462, MS 500i, and several other professional models. It replaces manual carburetor needle adjustments with an electronic fuel control valve that continuously optimizes the fuel/air mixture based on altitude, temperature, load, and RPM.

The MS 500i takes this further with an electronically controlled fuel injection system rather than a conventional carburetor, making it particularly sensitive to sensor and solenoid faults. Symptoms of M-Tronic faults include surging at part throttle, failure to reach maximum RPM, sudden rich or lean conditions, or complete refusal to self-adjust after altitude changes.

M-Tronic requires a learning cycle to establish baseline calibration. To reset: start the engine, allow it to warm for 2 minutes, then hold the throttle at WOT in open air (not under load) for 3 full seconds. Release and repeat twice. This forces the system to relearn its base fuel map. On the MS 500i, use Stihl's diagnostic tool (ISTA) for detailed fault code readout and sensor checks.

Battery voltage in the M-Tronic control module is maintained by the ignition generator. A weak ignition module output can corrupt the M-Tronic memory. If reset does not resolve surging or stalling, test the ignition coil output voltage with a multimeter before replacing the M-Tronic unit.

causes.cause: M-Tronic calibration lost due to fuel change, altitude change, or extended storage
Defective M-Tronic control solenoid or fuel valve
Weak ignition coil corrupting M-Tronic memory supply
Contaminated fuel sensor or throttle position input error

symptoms: Engine surges or hunts at part throttle
Chainsaw will not reach maximum RPM
Poor performance after fuel change or altitude change
Idle instability after extended storage
MS 500i has no spark or injector pulse

fixSteps.title: Perform the M-Tronic reset procedure
fixSteps.instruction: Start the engine and let it warm up for 2 minutes. Hold full throttle (WOT) with the chain brake engaged and the bar in open air for 3 continuous seconds. Release, wait 10 seconds, repeat twice. The system will relearn its fuel map.

fixSteps.title: Use fresh, correct-ratio fuel
fixSteps.instruction: Drain old fuel and refill with fresh 50:1 mix or Stihl MotoMix pre-mixed fuel. M-Tronic is sensitive to fuel quality and octane rating. Always use 89 octane or higher with no more than 10% ethanol.

fixSteps.title: Test and replace the ignition coil if needed
fixSteps.instruction: Use a multimeter to test ignition coil primary winding resistance (typically 0.3–1.2 ohms) and secondary winding (5-8 kohms). If readings are out of spec, replace the ignition coil — a weak coil can disrupt M-Tronic voltage.

fixSteps.title: Dealer diagnostic with ISTA tool (MS 500i)
fixSteps.instruction: For MS 500i persistent faults, only the Stihl ISTA PC diagnostic software can read sensor fault codes. Take the saw to an authorized Stihl dealer for ISTA diagnosis of the fuel injection module, throttle sensor, and control unit.

repairTime: 10 minutes for reset; 1–3 hours for component replacement
whenToCallPro: MS 500i fuel injection faults and M-Tronic control unit replacement require the Stihl ISTA diagnostic tool, which is only available at authorized Stihl dealers. Always start with the reset procedure before assuming the control unit has failed.
videoSearchQuery: stihl MS 271 MS 500i M-Tronic fault fix reset

faq.q: What chainsaws have M-Tronic?
faq.a: M-Tronic is available on the MS 271, MS 291, MS 362 C-M, MS 441 C-M, MS 462 C-M, and MS 500i, among other models. The 'M' suffix in model names indicates M-Tronic.

faq.q: Does M-Tronic eliminate the need for carburetor adjustment?
faq.a: Yes, for altitude and temperature compensation. The M-Tronic system replaces the H and L needle adjustments. However, the carburetor still requires periodic cleaning and diaphragm replacement.

faq.q: Will M-Tronic work with E15 or higher ethanol fuel?
faq.a: No. Stihl recommends a maximum of 10% ethanol (E10). Higher ethanol content can damage seals, corrode the carburetor, and cause the M-Tronic to establish an incorrect fuel map.

title: Primer Bulb Cracked, Hard, or Not Priming
shortDescription: The primer bulb is cracked, hardened, or collapsed, preventing fuel from reaching the carburetor for cold starts. A failed primer bulb causes hard starting and no-start conditions.
fullDescription:
The primer bulb (also called the purge bulb) on Stihl chainsaws draws fuel from the tank into the carburetor to assist cold starting. It is a small, dome-shaped rubber bulb mounted on the carburetor or airbox. When pressed and released repeatedly, it moves fuel through a check valve into the carburetor circuit, displacing stale air and providing a fuel-rich mixture for ignition.

Primer bulbs are made of rubber that degrades with age, heat, and fuel exposure — especially with ethanol-blended fuels. Symptoms of a failed bulb include hardness (does not spring back), visible cracking, collapse when pressed, or failure to fill with fuel when pressed. A cracked bulb draws air instead of fuel and can never prime properly.

The check valves associated with the primer circuit can also fail. There are typically two small flap valves inside the carburetor that allow fuel to flow in one direction only through the primer circuit. If these valves are stuck open or torn, the primer bulb will move in and out without drawing fuel.

Replacement is simple and inexpensive. Most Stihl primer bulbs cost under $5 and can be replaced without tools in under 10 minutes. Always replace the bulb when rebuilding the carburetor, as it is considered a wear item.

causes.cause: Aged or cracked rubber primer bulb from fuel and UV exposure
Failed primer circuit check valve in carburetor body
Clogged fuel filter preventing fuel from reaching the primer
Air leak at the primer bulb grommet or mounting

symptoms: Primer bulb feels hard or does not spring back when pressed
Visible cracks or splits on the primer bulb
No fuel visible inside the bulb when pressed
Hard starting even after multiple prime attempts
Chainsaw floods with fuel or bulb collapses inward

fixSteps.title: Inspect the primer bulb
fixSteps.instruction: Examine the primer bulb for cracks, hardening, or distortion. Press and release it — it should spring back quickly. Look through the clear or translucent bulb for visible fuel fill. If cracked, hard, or no fuel fills it, replace it.

fixSteps.title: Replace the primer bulb
fixSteps.instruction: Pry the old bulb off the carburetor nipple or unscrew the retaining ring. Install the new bulb, ensuring it seats fully on the nipple. Press several times to confirm fuel fills the bulb and the bulb springs back.

fixSteps.title: Inspect primer circuit check valves
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the carburetor and locate the primer check valves (small flaps or needle valves inside). Use carburetor cleaner to flush them. Replace the carburetor rebuild kit if any check valves are torn or stuck.

whenToCallPro: If replacing the primer bulb and cleaning check valves does not restore priming function, the carburetor body itself may be cracked or warped. Have a dealer inspect before purchasing a full carburetor replacement.
videoSearchQuery: stihl chainsaw primer bulb replacement fix

faq.q: How many times should I press the primer bulb before starting?
faq.a: Stihl recommends pressing the primer bulb 5-10 times until fuel is visible in the bulb. Over-priming can flood the engine.

faq.q: Can I start my Stihl chainsaw without the primer bulb?
faq.a: Yes, but cold starting is much harder. The engine can still draw fuel through the carburetor without a working primer, but you may need many more pull attempts.

faq.q: Why does my primer bulb collapse inward and stay collapsed?
faq.a: A primer bulb that collapses and does not return indicates the bulb has lost elasticity and must be replaced. It may also indicate the carburetor check valve is preventing fuel return.

title: Carburetor Clogged or Out of Adjustment
shortDescription: The carburetor is blocked with fuel varnish, the diaphragm is stiff, or idle/mixture screws are out of adjustment, causing rough running, stalling, or hard starting.
fullDescription:
The carburetor is the heart of the Stihl chainsaw fuel system. It meters fuel and air in precise ratios across all throttle positions. Stihl chainsaws use Walbro or Zama diaphragm-type carburetors, which are self-priming and can operate in any orientation. These carburetors contain a metering diaphragm, a pump diaphragm, multiple check valves, and a needle valve — all of which can fail with age, fuel contamination, or ethanol degradation.

Varnish deposits from evaporated stale fuel are the primary cause of carburetor failure. These deposits form in the tiny jets and passages of the carburetor when ethanol-blended fuel is left in the system. The deposits restrict or block fuel flow, causing lean running, stalling, and hard starting. Ultrasonic cleaning or a rebuild kit resolves most cases.

The metering diaphragm controls fuel delivery at all throttle positions. As it ages, it stiffens and loses flexibility, which reduces fuel volume to the engine. A stiff diaphragm causes the engine to run lean under load, stall at WOT, and have poor throttle response. This diaphragm must be replaced — cleaning does not restore elasticity.

Factory carburetor settings for Stihl chainsaws are typically: Low (L) screw = 1 turn out from gently seated; High (H) screw = 1 turn out. After cleaning, set these as base settings and adjust for correct idle and throttle response with a tachometer.

causes.cause: Varnish deposits from stale fuel blocking jets and passages
Stiff or cracked metering diaphragm
Incorrect H or L needle adjustment
Failed carburetor inlet needle valve
Warped or cracked carburetor body beyond cleaning

symptoms: Rough or erratic idle
Hard cold starting despite good spark and fresh fuel
Engine surges at part throttle
Rich running (black exhaust smoke, wet plug)
Lean running (engine runs hot, stalls under load)
Fuel leaks from carburetor body or gaskets

fixSteps.title: Remove and disassemble the carburetor
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the air filter, fuel lines, and throttle linkage. Remove the carburetor from the intake. Disassemble fully, noting orientation of all parts. Photograph before disassembly.

fixSteps.title: Clean all passages with carburetor cleaner
fixSteps.instruction: Spray carburetor cleaner through every jet and passage. Use a thin wire strand (not a drill bit) to clear blocked jets. Do not enlarge jet openings. Blow dry with compressed air.

fixSteps.title: Install carburetor rebuild kit
fixSteps.instruction: Replace metering diaphragm, pump diaphragm, gaskets, needle valve, and all o-rings from the rebuild kit. Install new check valve flaps if included. Reassemble in reverse order using the photograph as reference.

fixSteps.title: Set base adjustment and tune
fixSteps.instruction: Set L and H screws to 1 turn out from gently seated. Start the engine and let it warm fully. Adjust L screw for smooth idle, then H screw for maximum RPM without over-revving (use a tachometer). Final idle adjustment for chain not to move.

whenToCallPro: If the carburetor body is cracked, warped, or has stripped adjustment screw threads, replacement is more cost-effective than repair. A Stihl dealer can also perform professional ultrasonic cleaning if cleaning by hand does not resolve blockages.
videoSearchQuery: stihl chainsaw carburetor clean rebuild fix

faq.q: Do I need a special tool to adjust the Stihl carburetor screws?
faq.a: Yes. Stihl uses a D-shaped (Splined) adjustment screw head to deter tampering. A Stihl carb adjustment tool (part 5910 890 2001) or a compatible aftermarket tool is required. Standard screwdrivers will not fit.

faq.q: How do I know if I need a rebuild kit or a whole new carburetor?
faq.a: If the carburetor body is intact and not cracked, a rebuild kit is usually sufficient. If the body is warped from overheating or threads are stripped, replace the entire carburetor.

faq.q: Can I clean a carburetor without removing it?
faq.a: You can spray carb cleaner into the air intake while running to dissolve some deposits, but thorough cleaning requires removal and disassembly to access all internal jets and passages.

title: Spark Plug Fouled, Failed, or Incorrect Gap
shortDescription: The spark plug is fouled with carbon or oil, cracked, worn, or gapped incorrectly, preventing ignition of the fuel/air mixture.
fullDescription:
The spark plug is the most frequently replaced wear part on a Stihl chainsaw. It must deliver a strong spark at the precise moment of ignition, withstand extreme pressure and heat, and resist fouling from oil, carbon, and fuel deposits. On a 2-stroke chainsaw engine, the plug fires every revolution — far more frequently than in an automotive engine — accelerating wear.

Carbon fouling (dry black deposits) indicates a rich mixture from an over-rich carburetor setting, a choked airflow from a dirty air filter, or excessive idling. Oil fouling (wet, black, oily deposits) suggests worn piston rings or engine seal failure allowing oil into the combustion chamber. A white or light gray plug that has the tip eroded indicates an excessively lean mixture — this can cause catastrophic engine damage if not corrected.

The standard plug for most Stihl chainsaws is the NGK BPMR7A (Bosch WSB6F equivalent). Some high-performance models use a colder heat range plug. Always verify the correct plug for your model — using the wrong heat range causes premature fouling or pre-ignition damage.

Plug gap is critical. Most Stihl chainsaws require 0.5 mm (0.020 in). A gap that is too wide causes misfires and hard starting; a gap that is too narrow reduces spark energy. Always check the gap on a new plug before installation, even on new plugs from the factory.

causes.cause: Carbon fouling from rich mixture, dirty air filter, or excessive idling
Spark plug worn beyond service life (>100 hours or 1 season)
Incorrect plug gap causing weak or absent spark
Oil fouling from failed crankshaft seal or piston rings

symptoms: Hard starting or no-start condition
Engine misfires or runs rough
Loss of power under load
Spark plug is black and wet with fuel or oil
Spark plug electrode is eroded or cracked
Engine starts but shuts off after 30-60 seconds of running

fixSteps.title: Remove and inspect the spark plug
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the spark plug wire. Use a 19 mm spark plug wrench to unscrew the plug. Examine the electrode and insulator for fouling, wear, or cracking. Note the deposit color to diagnose root cause.

fixSteps.title: Gap and install new spark plug
fixSteps.instruction: Check gap on new NGK BPMR7A plug with a feeler gauge — should be 0.5 mm. Adjust if needed. Apply a small amount of anti-seize to threads. Install hand-tight, then 1/2 turn with wrench. Do not overtighten.

fixSteps.title: Verify spark quality
fixSteps.instruction: Connect an inline spark tester between the plug wire and the plug. Pull the starter rope. A bright blue spark confirms the ignition system is healthy. A weak yellow or no spark indicates ignition coil issues.

fixSteps.title: Address root cause of fouling
fixSteps.instruction: If the plug fouled rapidly after replacement, correct the underlying cause: adjust the carburetor for the correct mixture, clean or replace the air filter, or inspect for crankcase seal failure if oil fouling is present.

whenToCallPro: If the new plug fouls again within a few hours of use, especially with oil deposits, the engine has an internal problem (crankshaft seal, piston rings) that requires professional diagnosis and potentially a full engine rebuild.
videoSearchQuery: stihl chainsaw spark plug replacement fix

faq.q: How often should I replace the spark plug on my Stihl chainsaw?
faq.a: Stihl recommends replacing the spark plug at least once per season or every 100 hours of operation, whichever comes first. Inspect it every 25 hours.

faq.q: What does a white spark plug indicate on a Stihl?
faq.a: A white plug tip indicates a very lean fuel mixture or overheating. Running lean can cause severe engine damage. Richen the carburetor H screw and investigate for air leaks immediately.

faq.q: Can I clean and reuse a fouled spark plug?
faq.a: You can clean light carbon fouling with a wire brush and carburetor cleaner, but a new plug is more reliable. A severely fouled or oil-soaked plug should always be replaced rather than cleaned.

title: Flywheel Woodruff Key Sheared — Ignition Timing Lost
shortDescription: The flywheel Woodruff key has sheared, allowing the flywheel to rotate out of position and causing incorrect ignition timing, which results in hard starting, kickback, or no-start.
fullDescription:
The flywheel Woodruff key is a small, crescent-shaped metal key that indexes the flywheel to the crankshaft at the exact rotational position required for correct ignition timing. It is deliberately made of soft metal (aluminum on most models) to act as a sacrificial part — if the engine kicks back violently, or the chain strikes an obstruction while under full power, the soft key shears in preference to bending the crankshaft.

When the key shears, the flywheel rotates freely on the crankshaft taper and settles in a random position. This throws off the ignition timing by several degrees, causing the spark to fire at the wrong point in the piston stroke. The result is typically very hard starting, severe backfire through the starter rope (kickback at the pull cord), weak power, or complete inability to start.

Diagnosing a sheared key: if the engine previously started normally but now kicks back violently at the starter or refuses to fire after a sudden impact or chain strike, suspect the flywheel key. Remove the flywheel and inspect the key in its keyway — a sheared key will show an obvious break or deformation.

Replacement is straightforward but requires flywheel removal tools. The flywheel is held by a left-hand thread nut on the crankshaft (clockwise to loosen on most models). A flywheel puller is essential — do not pry or strike the flywheel, as this can damage the crankshaft taper. New keys are inexpensive ($2-$5) and restoration of correct timing is immediate.

causes.cause: Violent engine kickback or chain strike causing flywheel rotation
Starter rope seized or jammed causing sudden crankshaft stop
Previous improper flywheel installation without key seated

symptoms: Violent kickback through the starter rope
Engine refuses to start after a chain strike or jam
Engine starts but has very poor power and knocks
Backfire through the exhaust during starting attempts
Previously ran well, now suddenly won't start

fixSteps.title: Remove the flywheel cover and access the flywheel nut
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the starter assembly and flywheel cover. Install the piston stop tool through the spark plug hole. The flywheel nut is typically a LEFT-HAND thread on Stihl — turn CLOCKWISE to loosen. Use the correct socket (usually 19 mm).

fixSteps.title: Remove the flywheel with a puller
fixSteps.instruction: Thread the flywheel puller into the flywheel's puller holes. Turn the center bolt clockwise to press against the crankshaft end and pop the flywheel off the taper. Never pry or strike the flywheel.

fixSteps.title: Inspect and replace the Woodruff key
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the sheared key pieces from the crankshaft keyway. Clean the keyway. Install the new Woodruff key, ensuring it sits fully in the keyway groove and is not proud. Verify the flywheel slides onto the crankshaft with the key aligned to the flywheel keyway.

fixSteps.title: Reinstall flywheel and verify timing
fixSteps.instruction: Slide the flywheel onto the crankshaft with the key aligned. Install the nut and torque to spec (RIGHT-HAND direction — counterclockwise to tighten on left-hand thread). Set the ignition coil air gap to 0.2 mm. Reinstall starter and test.

whenToCallPro: If the crankshaft taper is scored or the flywheel taper bore is damaged from the key shearing, the crankshaft may need replacement — a task requiring full engine disassembly best left to a Stihl dealer.
videoSearchQuery: stihl chainsaw flywheel woodruff key sheared timing fix

faq.q: Why is the Woodruff key made of soft metal?
faq.a: The soft aluminum key is designed to shear when the engine experiences a sudden shock, protecting the crankshaft from bending or other serious damage. It is a built-in sacrificial component.

faq.q: Can a sheared flywheel key cause the starter rope to kick back?
faq.a: Yes. With timing advanced beyond TDC, the spark fires before the piston reaches top dead center, creating a backward pressure that can kick the starter rope violently. This is a telltale sign of a sheared key.

faq.q: How do I know which direction the flywheel nut turns on my Stihl?
faq.a: Most Stihl chainsaws have a LEFT-HAND threaded flywheel nut, meaning you turn it CLOCKWISE to loosen. Always verify in your model's service manual before applying force.

title: Recoil Starter Assembly Broken or Rope Jammed
shortDescription: The recoil starter rope is broken, frayed, will not rewind, or the starter pawl mechanism has failed, preventing the engine from being cranked.
fullDescription:
The recoil starter is the pull-cord mechanism that cranks the chainsaw engine. It consists of a rope, rope pulley, recoil spring (also called a return spring or rewind spring), and a set of starter pawls or dogs that engage the flywheel hub on the pull stroke and disengage on the return. Failure of any of these components renders the chainsaw impossible to start.

Rope breakage is the most common failure and typically occurs at the handle knot or where the rope passes through the pulley. The break happens from fatigue after thousands of pull cycles combined with weathering. A broken rope is the easiest repair — replacement takes 15-20 minutes and costs under $5 for the rope.

The recoil spring can break or pop out of its housing. When the spring breaks, the rope pays out but does not rewind. When the spring uncoils from the housing (common after aggressive pulls), the rope goes limp and the pulley cannot be turned. A new recoil spring must be pre-wound and carefully installed — a released coil spring can cause injury. Wear eye protection during spring installation.

The starter pawls (dogs or ratchets) can wear, break, or lose spring tension. Worn pawls slip over the flywheel hub rather than engaging it, producing a characteristic clicking sound without cranking. Pawl replacement is part of a complete starter assembly rebuild. New starter assemblies are available as a unit for most models for $20-$40, making replacement more practical than rebuilding individual components.

causes.cause: Broken or frayed starter rope from wear and fatigue
Broken or uncoiled recoil spring
Worn or broken starter pawls failing to engage flywheel
Rope pulley cracked or jammed with debris

symptoms: Starter rope snaps or is pulled out with no resistance
Rope pulls out but does not rewind back into housing
Clicking sound when pulling rope but engine does not crank
Rope feels locked and cannot be pulled
Rope retracts slowly or incompletely

fixSteps.title: Remove the starter assembly
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the 4-5 bolts (Torx T27) holding the starter housing to the engine. Lift the assembly away. Identify whether the rope, spring, or pawls have failed before disassembly.

fixSteps.title: Replace the starter rope
fixSteps.instruction: Thread new 3.5 mm (No. 3) starter rope through the pulley and handle. Tie a square knot at each end. Wind the pulley 2-3 additional turns against the spring before threading through the housing guide hole to ensure proper spring tension and full retraction.

fixSteps.title: Replace the recoil spring
fixSteps.instruction: WEAR EYE PROTECTION. Remove the rope pulley. Carefully remove the old spring. Pre-wind the new spring into its cup or carrier. Install the carrier into the housing. Do not let the spring release freely — it can cause injury.

fixSteps.title: Replace complete starter assembly
fixSteps.instruction: If spring, pawls, and rope are all worn, replace the complete starter assembly rather than individual parts. A complete assembly costs $20-$40 and takes 10 minutes to bolt on.

whenToCallPro: If the starter rope is locked and cannot be pulled at all, the engine may be seized or the chain brake may be engaged. Verify chain brake release before attempting rope repair. A seized engine requires professional diagnosis.
videoSearchQuery: stihl chainsaw recoil starter rope replacement fix

faq.q: What thickness starter rope do Stihl chainsaws use?
faq.a: Most Stihl chainsaws use 3.5 mm (No. 3) round starter rope. Larger professional models may use 4 mm rope. Check the existing rope diameter or your model's parts list for the correct size.

faq.q: Why does my Stihl starter rope not retract all the way?
faq.a: Insufficient spring tension. When replacing the rope, pre-wind the pulley 2-3 full turns against spring tension before threading the rope through the housing exit hole. This stores enough tension to fully retract the rope.

faq.q: Can a starter assembly from one Stihl model fit another?
faq.a: Starter assemblies are model-specific due to differences in housing bolt patterns and rope pulley size. Always source the correct assembly for your exact model number.

title: Decompression Valve Stuck Open or Leaking
shortDescription: The decompression valve is stuck in the open or closed position, making starting excessively hard or preventing the engine from reaching full compression under load.
fullDescription:
Stihl's larger chainsaws — including the MS 271, MS 291, MS 391, MS 462, and MS 500i — are equipped with a decompression valve (also called an Easy2Start or decomp valve) to reduce starter pull force. When pressed before starting, this small valve vents compression from the cylinder, making the pull stroke much easier. The valve automatically closes when the engine fires and compression builds.

If the decompression valve sticks in the open position, compression never builds in the cylinder and the engine cannot fire. The symptom is an unusually easy pull with no start — the rope offers almost no resistance. Conversely, if the valve is stuck closed and cannot be pressed, the pull force required to crank the engine is extremely high, risking starter rope breakage or user injury on larger displacement saws.

Carbon buildup inside the valve body is the most common cause of sticking. The valve stem can become coated in carbon deposits from combustion gases, preventing it from seating or from retracting. Soaking in penetrating oil and working the valve stem repeatedly resolves light carbon fouling. Severe fouling requires valve removal and cleaning or replacement.

The valve seat can also crack or corrode, causing a continuous compression leak even when the valve is not depressed. This results in noticeably reduced power and difficulty starting that cannot be resolved by carburetor adjustment. Test for a leaking valve by applying pressure to the spark plug hole and listening for air escape at the valve location.

causes.cause: Carbon buildup on valve stem preventing proper seating
Valve stuck open due to corrosion or debris in valve body
Cracked or damaged valve seat causing continuous compression leak
Failed valve spring allowing valve to remain open at full throttle

symptoms: Rope pulls with almost no resistance and engine will not fire
Engine cranks very hard with the decomp button stuck in
Noticeable loss of power and compression on large models
Hissing sound from cylinder area during pull start
Engine starts but runs rough and lacks full power

fixSteps.title: Test decompression valve function
fixSteps.instruction: With the saw off, press and release the decompression valve button. It should depress with light thumb pressure and snap back when released. Pull the rope — it should feel easier with the valve pressed. If no difference in pull effort, the valve is stuck open.

fixSteps.title: Clean a stuck valve stem
fixSteps.instruction: Spray penetrating oil (e.g. WD-40 or PB Blaster) into the valve body around the stem. Work the stem in and out repeatedly with a small pick or screwdriver. Use compressed air to blow out loosened carbon. Repeat until the valve moves freely and snaps back.

fixSteps.title: Remove and inspect the decompression valve
fixSteps.instruction: Using the correct valve removal socket or an adjustable wrench, unscrew the decompression valve from the cylinder. Inspect the valve seat and stem for cracks, corrosion, or carbon. Clean thoroughly. Replace the valve if the seat or stem is damaged.
tools: Decompression valve socket or adjustable wrench

fixSteps.title: Test compression after repair
fixSteps.instruction: Install a compression gauge in the spark plug hole. Pull the rope sharply 5 times with the valve closed (button not pressed). Compare reading to spec for your model — typically 130-170 PSI. Low compression after valve repair indicates a separate engine problem.

whenToCallPro: If compression remains low after decompression valve replacement, the cylinder, piston rings, or valves may be worn — have a Stihl dealer perform a full compression and leakdown test before further repair.
videoSearchQuery: stihl chainsaw decompression valve stuck fix

faq.q: Should I always press the decompression valve before starting?
faq.a: Yes, on models equipped with one. Pressing the valve before each start reduces pull effort significantly and reduces stress on the recoil starter and rope. Release the valve after the engine fires.

faq.q: My Stihl MS 391 is very hard to pull — could it be the decomp valve?
faq.a: Yes. If the decompression valve button is stuck in the inward (closed) position and cannot be pressed, the engine will be at full compression making the rope very difficult to pull. Try cleaning the valve stem first.

faq.q: Is the decompression valve the same as a compression release?
faq.a: Yes. Decompression valve, compression release valve, and Easy2Start valve all refer to the same component. They reduce compression during the starting pull, then automatically seal once the engine fires.

faq.q: How do I know if my model has a decompression valve?
faq.a: Look for a small button or nipple on top of the cylinder head, near the spark plug. Models without one include the MS 170 and MS 180. The MS 271, MS 291, MS 391, MS 462, and MS 500i all have one.
